summary: Azure Kubernetes Service node pools and clusters, plus Container Registry. Node-count and tier scaling are self-service cost knobs; disabling registry public network access is a guardrailed hardening move; provisioning a new cluster, registry, or container instance routes to an engineer.

operations.title: Update AKS node pool tags
operations.summary: Set or update this node pool's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone.
operations.description: Sets or merges tags on an AKS node pool. Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on running nodes or pods.

params.name: node_pool
params.label: AKS node pool
params.help: The node pool to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Azure tag names cannot contain < > % & \ ? /.

decisions: Tags are metadata only — cost-neutral and freely reversible — so this stays LOW risk.

operations.title: Scale an AKS node pool's node count
operations.summary: Change how many nodes run in this pool. Kubernetes drains and reschedules pods as nodes are added or removed.
operations.description: Sets node_count on an AKS node pool, changing how many nodes it runs. Scale-out provisions new nodes (a cost delta); scale-in cordons and drains nodes before removing them, so Kubernetes reschedules affected pods onto the remaining capacity. Has no effect on a pool with cluster-autoscaler enabled, since Azure recomputes node_count itself there.

params.name: node_pool
params.label: AKS node pool
params.help: The node pool to scale.

params.name: new_node_count
params.label: Node count
params.help: Node count to run in this pool. Floored at 1 so a pool is never emptied outright; above the policy cap of 100 nodes the request routes to an engineer.

decisions: Cost-changing — each node is billed compute — so MEDIUM floor.
Scale-in relies on Kubernetes' normal cordon/drain/reschedule behavior for evicted pods rather than an instance-level reboot, so downtime stays none at the cluster level.
A floor of 1 keeps this safe for both system and user pools; a system pool cannot run at 0 nodes.
No effect on a pool with cluster-autoscaler enabled — Azure manages node_count itself there.

operations.title: Update AKS cluster tags
operations.summary: Set or update this cluster's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone.
operations.description: Sets or merges tags on the AKS cluster resource. Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on nodes or workloads.

params.name: cluster
params.label: AKS cluster
params.help: The AKS cluster to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Azure tag names cannot contain < > % & \ ? /.

decisions: Tags are metadata only — cost-neutral and freely reversible — so this stays LOW risk.

operations.title: Change the AKS cluster's SKU tier
operations.summary: Switch between the Free and Standard control-plane tiers. Standard adds an uptime SLA at extra cost.
operations.description: Sets sku_tier on the AKS cluster, switching its control-plane tier between Free (no SLA) and Standard (a financially-backed uptime SLA, recommended for production). No node or workload disruption — only the managed control plane's service tier changes. Premium (the AKS Long Term Support tier) is excluded here because it also requires a support_plan selection that this scalar op does not capture; a move to Premium routes to an engineer.

params.name: cluster
params.label: AKS cluster
params.help: The AKS cluster to change.

params.name: new_sku_tier
params.label: New SKU tier
params.help: Free has no uptime SLA; Standard adds a financially-backed SLA for production clusters. Premium needs an additional support_plan choice and is engineer-only.

decisions: Cost-changing — Standard bills a per-cluster SLA fee — so MEDIUM floor.
Only the managed control plane's tier changes; nodes and running workloads are untouched, so downtime stays none.

Premium is excluded from the allowlist — it needs a companion support_plan attribute this single-attribute op does not set, so it is routed to an engineer instead of risking a partially-configured Premium tier.

operations.title: Update Container Registry tags
operations.summary: Set or update this registry's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone.
operations.description: Sets or merges tags on the Container Registry. Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on stored images or running pulls/pushes.

params.name: registry
params.label: Container registry
params.help: The registry to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Azure tag names cannot contain < > % & \ ? /.

decisions: Tags are metadata only — cost-neutral and freely reversible — so this stays LOW risk.

operations.title: Change the Container Registry SKU
operations.summary: Move this registry between Basic, Standard, and Premium. Premium adds geo-replication and private networking support.
operations.description: Sets sku on the Container Registry, moving it between Basic, Standard, and Premium. Applies online with no registry downtime; Premium unlocks geo-replication, customer-managed keys, and private endpoints, while a move down to Basic/Standard drops those capabilities if they were in use.

params.name: registry
params.label: Container registry
params.help: The registry to resize.

params.name: new_sku
params.label: New SKU
params.help: Basic is dev/test scale; Standard raises storage and throughput; Premium adds geo-replication, private endpoints, and customer-managed keys.

decisions: Cost-changing — Premium is materially more expensive than Basic/Standard — so MEDIUM floor.
Applies online; pulls and pushes in flight are not interrupted, so downtime stays none.
Moving down from Premium silently drops geo-replication/private-endpoint/customer-managed-key features if they were configured — flagged here since the op itself does not check for that dependency.

operations.title: Disable public network access on a Container Registry
operations.summary: Cut the registry off from the public internet, leaving only private/network-rule access. One-way hardening; an engineer re-opens it.
operations.description: Sets public_network_access_enabled to false on the Container Registry, blocking access over the public internet and leaving only private endpoints or configured network rules reachable. Tightening-only — this op can only turn public access off. If nothing private is wired up yet, this can cut off pulls and pushes that currently rely on the public endpoint, so confirm a private path exists first.

params.name: registry
params.label: Container registry
params.help: The registry to lock down.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: Set to false to block the registry's public endpoint. This op cannot be used to turn public access back on.

decisions: Network-reachability change — HIGH floor per the general risk doctrine, even though it only ever tightens.
Can break pulls/pushes that depend on the public endpoint if no private endpoint or network rule is wired up yet — verify a private path exists before applying.
Tighten-only posture — the allowlist offers only false; re-opening public access is not offered here and needs an engineer.

operations.title: Provision a new AKS cluster
operations.summary: Request a brand-new Kubernetes cluster; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it is applied.
operations.description: Stand up a new AKS cluster as a complete specification: the default node pool's size and count, cluster identity, and the estate's RBAC, local-account, and API-server exposure guardrails. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: cluster_name
params.label: Cluster name
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes, like aks-checkout-01. Names the cluster and the generated draft's Terraform block.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group the cluster is created in, chosen from the estate inventory.

params.name: dns_prefix
params.label: DNS prefix
params.help: Prefixes the cluster's managed DNS name and API server FQDN. Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes.

params.name: kubernetes_version
params.label: Kubernetes version
params.help: The engineer picks and validates the exact patch version during review, against the estate's supported-version policy.

params.name: node_pool_name
params.label: Default node pool name
params.help: Names the cluster's default node pool. Lower-case letters and numbers only, 1—12 characters — an AKS naming limit.

params.name: vm_size
params.label: Node size
params.help: VM size for every node in the default pool. D-series is general purpose, E-series memory-optimized, F-series compute-optimized, B-series burstable for dev/test.

params.name: node_count
params.label: Node count
params.help: Starting node count for the default pool. Floored at 1; above the policy cap of 100 nodes the request routes to an engineer.

params.name: auto_scaling_enabled
params.label: Enable node pool autoscaling
params.help: Leave unset for a fixed-size pool. Set true and add a minimum/maximum count to let Azure scale the pool itself.

params.name: min_count
params.label: Minimum node count (autoscale)
params.help: Only used when autoscaling is enabled. Leave blank for a fixed-size pool.

params.name: max_count
params.label: Maximum node count (autoscale)
params.help: Only used when autoscaling is enabled. Leave blank for a fixed-size pool.

params.name: identity_type
params.label: Managed identity
params.help: Always a system-assigned managed identity for a new cluster — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: role_based_access_control_enabled
params.label: Kubernetes RBAC
params.help: Always on for a new cluster — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: local_account_disabled
params.label: Local admin accounts
params.help: Local (non-Azure-AD) cluster admin accounts are always disabled for a new cluster — written into the draft, never asked. Requires Azure AD group integration; see the review decisions.

params.name: private_cluster_enabled
params.label: Private cluster
params.help: true keeps the Kubernetes API server off the public internet (needs a private network path to reach it); false publishes it — pair that with authorized IP ranges during review.

params.name: azure_policy_enabled
params.label: Azure Policy add-on
params.help: Always on for a new cluster — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Cluster name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this cluster runs
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this cluster.

decisions: Kubernetes version — the engineer picks and validates the exact patch version against the estate's supported-version policy.
Disabling local accounts requires Azure AD group integration (azure_active_directory_role_based_access_control) added during review, or cluster access is lost.
Node pool autoscaling — leave the minimum/maximum blank for a fixed-size pool, or set them (with autoscaling enabled) to let Azure manage node_count itself.

Private cluster networking — confirm a private connectivity path (VPN, ExpressRoute, jump host) exists before relying on a private API server; authorized IP ranges are the alternative for a public-but-restricted API server.

operations.title: Provision a new container registry
operations.summary: Request a brand-new container registry; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it is applied.
operations.description: Stand up a new Container Registry as a complete specification: SKU tier plus the estate's admin-access and network-exposure guardrails. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: registry_name
params.label: Registry name
params.help: Letters and numbers only, 5—50 characters — no dashes or dots. Becomes part of the registry's login server address and must be globally unique across Azure.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group the registry is created in, chosen from the estate inventory.

params.name: sku
params.label: SKU
params.help: Basic is dev/test scale; Standard raises storage and throughput; Premium adds geo-replication, private endpoints, and customer-managed keys.

params.name: admin_enabled
params.label: Admin user account
params.help: Always off for a new registry — written into the draft, never asked. Pulls and pushes authenticate with Azure AD / managed identity instead of a shared admin password.

params.name: anonymous_pull_enabled
params.label: Anonymous pull
params.help: Always off for a new registry — written into the draft, never asked. No image can be pulled without authentication.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: false keeps the registry reachable only over a private endpoint or configured network rules; true opens the public endpoint — pair that with IP network rules during review.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Registry name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this registry holds
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this registry.

decisions: Public network access defaults to off; if the registry genuinely needs a public endpoint, add IP network rules (network_rule_set) during review rather than leaving it open to any address.
Geo-replication, customer-managed keys, and private endpoints are Premium-tier follow-ups, not part of this baseline.

operations.title: Provision a new container instance
operations.summary: Request a brand-new container instance; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it is applied.
operations.description: Stand up a new Container Instance as a complete specification: the container image approach, sizing, one exposed port, and network exposure. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: group_name
params.label: Container group name
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes, like batch-worker-01. Names the container group and the generated draft's Terraform block.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group the container instance is created in, chosen from the estate inventory.

params.name: os_type
params.label: Operating system
params.help: The OS family for every container in this group; it cannot be mixed within one group.

params.name: container_name
params.label: Container name
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes. Names the single container this group runs.

params.name: image
params.label: Container image
params.help: Placeholder written into the draft so the required container.image renders inside the block — the engineer replaces <registry>/<image>:<tag> with the real, validated image reference during review (approved images follow the account standard).

params.name: cpu
params.label: CPU (cores)
params.help: vCPU cores reserved for the container, 0.5-4.

params.name: memory
params.label: Memory (GiB)
params.help: Memory reserved for the container in GiB, 0.5-16.

params.name: container_port
params.label: Exposed port
params.help: The single TCP port this container exposes. Additional ports are an engineer follow-up during review.

params.name: container_protocol
params.label: Port protocol
params.help: Always TCP for the exposed port — written into the draft, never asked. A UDP port is an engineer follow-up.

params.name: ip_address_type
params.label: IP address type
params.help: Private and None keep this off the public internet; Private additionally needs a subnet reference added during review. Public assigns a routable IP with no extra wiring.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Container instance name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this container instance runs
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this container instance.

decisions: Container image — the draft carries a <registry>/<image>:<tag> placeholder inside the container block; the engineer replaces it with the real, validated image reference during review.
Private IP address type needs the reviewing engineer to add a subnet reference (subnet_ids); None needs no networking; Public needs neither but is internet-reachable.
Only one exposed port is captured here; additional ports are an engineer follow-up during review.
Restart policy and diagnostics are left at the provider default; adjust during review if the workload needs different behavior.
